diagnosticQuestions.prompt: Many traders in the Wizards interviews cap risk per trade near:
choices: A small slice of equity (often discussed around ~1–2% per trade as a ballpark idea)
diagnosticQuestions.hint: Survival is the prerequisite for edge to matter.

diagnosticQuestions.prompt: After three losses, “doubling size to get even” mainly violates:
choices: Emotional discipline and sound risk management
diagnosticQuestions.hint: Revenge trading is a classic failure mode.

teachSlides.title: Edge without survival is zero
teachSlides.body: A good method is useless if one bad streak wipes you out. Position size and max loss rules keep you in the game.

teachSlides.title: Doing nothing is a skill
teachSlides.body: When there’s no setup, pros often wait. Sideways markets reward patience more than activity.

teachSlides.title: One methodology
teachSlides.body: Jumping between systems mid-stream destroys feedback. Master one playbook before collecting more.

recapSlide.title: Recap
recapSlide.body: Small risk per trade, no revenge sizing, patience when there’s no edge, and consistency in your process — the boring stuff that lets winners matter.

masteryQuestions.prompt: Risking a large fraction of equity on one trade is dangerous mainly because:
choices: A few bad outcomes can destroy the account
masteryQuestions.explanation: Drawdown math is brutal; survival first.

masteryQuestions.prompt: After a losing streak, the disciplined response is often:
choices: Return to rules, reduce size if needed, and avoid revenge trades
Enough reps with one system to know what works and what doesn’t
Never changing a rule even when wrong
masteryQuestions.explanation: You need clean data from a stable process to improve.
